serving.natural_language_portion: One standard set meal serving of approximately 550 g, comprising a bowl of braised beef rice noodles (approximately 400 g), a side of pickled radish (approximately 50 g), and a choice of drink (approximately 100 g liquid component counted minimally).

nutrient_source.title: Braised Beef Rice Noodles — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries data_source='hpb' and provenance='canonical' with confidence 0.95 across all per_100g fields. Values are accepted as sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo for braised beef rice noodles. The set meal includes a drink and pickled radish side; per_100g values reflect the composite dish as recorded by HPB.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Rice noodles, boiled (analogous estimate)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for braised beef rice noodle set as a composite dish. GI of 61 is estimated from rice noodles (boiled) as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care), which lists rice noodles at GI ~61 (medium). The braised beef component contributes negligible carbohydrate and does not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (61) × available carbohydrate per serving (138.5 g, based on 550 g × 25.19 g/100g) / 100 = 84.5. This is a high GL due to the large serving size; individual noodle bowl GL would be lower at approximately 400 g serving: GL = 61 × 100.76 / 100 ≈ 61.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g with favourable potassium offset (327 mg/100 g); braising broth and soy sauce may add further sodium beyond the per-100 g figure
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced broth and sauce to limit sodium intake, and rely on the favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio (1.41:1) to partially offset cardiovascular sodium load; total set meal sodium may exceed 500 mg and should be counted against the daily 2000 mg DASH target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice noodles carry a medium GI (~61) and the large set meal serving produces a high glycemic load (~61–85 GL units); dietary fibre at 3.7 g/100 g partially moderates postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to approximately 200–250 g of noodles, pair with the pickled radish side to add fibre, avoid sweetened drink options in the set, and monitor postprandial glucose given the medium-to-high glycemic load of a full serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Braised beef (red meat) contributes saturated fat and arachidonic acid (omega-6); total fat at 4.21 g/100 g is moderate but saturated fat breakdown is not reported; sodium at 231 mg/100 g adds cardiovascular sodium burden
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit red meat consumption frequency, request less braising sauce to reduce sodium and saturated fat exposure, and consider substituting beef with a leaner protein option where available; iron at 2.55 mg/100 g is a nutritional benefit but does not offset the red meat cardiovascular risk at regular consumption.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Braised beef is red meat high in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies inflammatory pathways via TNF-alpha and IL-6
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid regular consumption of braised beef dishes due to the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid content of red meat; substituting with chicken or tofu-based noodle dishes is recommended for RA management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Signature Braised Beef Rice Noodle Set (招牌红烧牛肉米线套餐) is a popular Chinese-style set meal served in Singapore, comprising a bowl of braised beef rice noodles (红烧牛肉米线), a side of pickled radish (爽口脆萝卜), and a choice of drink. The rice noodles are smooth, slightly chewy, and made from rice flour, providing a gluten-light base with a medium glycemic index of approximately 61. The beef is slow-braised in a soy sauce-based broth with aromatics such as star anise, cinnamon, and doubanjiang, producing a rich, savoury, umami-forward flavour with tender, fall-apart meat. Per 100 g, the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, 25.19 g carbohydrate, 3.7 g dietary fibre, 231 mg sodium, and 327 mg potassium. The fibre content moderates the carbohydrate load, and the potassium-to-sodium ratio of 1.41:1 is favourable for blood pressure management. Clinically, the dish is conditionally suitable for hypertension and Type 2 Diabetes with portion control and sauce reduction, but is a moderate cardiovascular concern due to red meat content and sodium from braising liquid. It is not recommended for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid profile of beef. The set meal is commonly consumed at lunch and dinner in Singapore hawker centres and Chinese noodle restaurants.

confidence_reason: All per_100g nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with provenance='canonical' and confidence=0.95 across all fields. The input record carries review_status='reviewed' and enrichment_source='manual'. GI is estimated from the closest approved analogous food (rice noodles, boiled) from the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the standard approved GI source. Multilingual names are provided for all mandatory Singaporean dish languages.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this composite braised beef rice noodle set are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry as of 2026-04-16. 2. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source; this is confirmed zero per the HPB Foodo database entry, which may reflect the composite dish average including the noodle base diluting the beef cholesterol contribution — this value should be verified against the full HPB record. 3. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate based on rice noodles (boiled) from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement exists for this composite set meal. 4. The set meal includes a choice of drink (three options); nutrient values reflect the noodle dish component as recorded by HPB and may not fully account for the drink selection. 5. Halal status is not confirmed; the dish contains beef which requires halal certification verification with the specific vendor. 6. Serving size of 550 g is an estimate for the full set meal; the noodle bowl alone is estimated at approximately 400 g.
